version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/9] memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the way memory cgroup (memcg) charges kmem pages and slab objects against per-node object cgroups (objcgs). It introduces a new helper function __current_obj_cgroup(int nid) that allows callers to specify the target node for the objcg lookup. The patch then uses this helper in two places: __memcg_kmem_charge_page() and __memcg_slab_post_alloc_hook(). This change ensures that kmem pages and slab objects are charged against the correct per-node objcg, even when the allocating CPU's node differs from the allocation's node.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/9] mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a metadata charge size calculation error in the Linux kernel's percpu code. The function pcpu_obj_full_size() was using an incorrect size for the obj_exts metadata, which is a vector of struct pcpuobj_ext. The fix involves changing the size calculation to use sizeof(struct pcpuobj_ext) instead of sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *). This patch addresses a bug introduced in commit 8f30d2660a38 and is part of a larger series of patches.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/9] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to handle per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. The first step, obj_cgroup_precharge(), reserves pages for memcg limit enforcement without updating kmem stats. The second step, obj_cgroup_account_kmem(), accounts kmem after the allocation succeeds and places any remaining sub-page remainder in the object stock. This decoupling allows temporary overcharging of memcg but ensures that things return to normal through obj_stock draining.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/9] mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node obj_cgroup objects to track MEMCG_KMEM statistics for NUMA nodes. It removes the MEMCG_KMEM field and wires generic functions to use the new per-node accounting. The patch does not convert kmem users to proper per-memcg-per-node accounting, which is done in subsequent patches. This change aims to improve memory management and tracking on systems with multiple NUMA nodes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 6/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces per-node kmem accounting for percpu allocations. It decouples the memcg charging from kmem accounting, allowing the post-alloc hook to account pages on their actual node rather than just the best-effort placement node. The patch also optimizes the accounting process by batching per node instead of per page, reducing the number of memcg work operations proportional to the number of nodes. Additionally, it precharges enough pages to cover the worst-case scenario where the allocation spans all nodes, ensuring that the per-node credit never runs short.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 7/9] mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata in the Linux kernel's mm/percpu.c file. The obj_exts array is vmalloc'd and its pages may reside on different nodes, so it walks these pages to add each page's slice to the correct node's byte accumulation. This ensures that each node is rounded up to whole pages only once, across payload and metadata combined.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 8/9] mm: percpu: skip the per-cpu node walk on single-node systems
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the per-cpu memory allocation by skipping a time-consuming walk of possible CPU nodes on single-node systems. The existing code would perform this walk to determine the NUMA node for each backing page, but since all pages are already on the same node in a single-node system, this step is unnecessary and only adds overhead. A new fast path has been added to pcpu_memcg_accumulate() that directly attributes the allocation to the first online node when there's only one online node, thus avoiding the O(num_possible_cpus) walk.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 9/9] mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates zswap and zsmalloc to use per-node object cgroup (obj_cgroup) for kernel memory (kmem) accounting, attributing compressed page charges to the correct NUMA node. However, it does not correctly handle entries that straddle pages across multiple nodes, which will be addressed in a separate series by Joshua Hahn. The patch adds a function `zs_handle_to_nid` to determine the NUMA node for a given zsmalloc handle and updates the `zswap_store_page` function to use this information. This change is necessary to ensure accurate kmem accounting for compressed pages.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that zs_handle_to_nid() does not acquire pool->lock like other handle-to-zspage paths, potentially causing a race condition between zswap's uncharge path and zspage migration.
review_comments.raw_body:
Does this need the same locking as the other handle-to-zspage paths?
zs_free() takes pool->lock before handle_to_obj() because zspage migration can
update or move the object behind the handle. This helper does the same decode
without the lock, so zswap's uncharge path can race migration and charge or
uncharge the wrong node, or observe transient zspage state.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Nhat Pham suggested charging compressed page charges to the page's node for now, and then revisiting the correct attribution once Joshua's patch series is in.
review_comments.raw_body:
Can we just charge it to the page's node for now? Once Joshua's patch
series is in, we can correctly charge the node owning the data :)

FWIW, this is how these zswap entries are organized in the LRU too -
following to the OG page's node.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch converts the task_struct's objcg field to a per-node array of obj_cgroup pointers, allowing for efficient and accurate accounting of memory usage across multiple NUMA nodes. The change addresses issues with current_obj_cgroup() returning an objcg whose nid did not match the current CPU's node, defeating per-node vmstat batching and accounting hierarchy. The patch also introduces a new tagged pointer to store the array, ensuring atomic updates and preserving existing race-with-kmem_attach semantics.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/9] per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 1/9] memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the task_struct's objcg field to a per-node array of obj_cgroup pointers, allowing for more efficient and accurate memory accounting across multiple NUMA nodes. The current_obj_cgroup() function is updated to index this array by numa_node_id(), and the current_objcg_update() function refreshes all entries in the array under an RCU read lock. The patch also introduces a new tagged pointer format for the obj_cgroup array, which preserves existing semantics with kmem_attach().

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 2/9] memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the way memory cgroup (memcg) charges kmem pages and slab objects against per-node object cgroups (objcgs). It introduces a new helper function __current_obj_cgroup() that takes a node ID as an argument, allowing for more accurate charging of memory against the correct node's objcg. The patch also updates callers in the kmem accounting path to use this new helper function, ensuring that per-node charge attribution and vmstat batching are correctly routed to the intended sibling of the same memcg. This change addresses a problem where allocations on one node were being charged against an objcg whose nid did not match the allocation's physical node, leading to incorrect charging and attribution.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 3/9] mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a metadata charge size calculation error in the Linux kernel's percpu code. The function pcpu_obj_full_size was using the wrong size for the obj_exts metadata, which is actually an array of struct pcpuobj_ext rather than a single struct obj_cgroup pointer. The fix involves changing the size calculation to use sizeof(struct pcpuobj_ext) instead of sizeof(struct obj_cgroup *). This patch addresses a problem introduced by a previous commit that added support for profiling memory allocations.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v2 4/9] mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits memory group (memcg) charging and kernel memory (kmem) accounting into two separate steps to handle per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting. It introduces new functions `obj_cgroup_precharge` and `obj_cgroup_account_kmem` to decouple the page counter charge from KMEM stats, allowing for temporary overcharging of memcgs during allocation. The patch also updates existing functions in `mm/memcontrol.c` and `include/linux/memcontrol.h` to reflect this change.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve kmem accounting by introducing per-memcg-per-node accounting, which reduces the overhead of discovering each backing page's real node in a many-CPU system. The patches batch memcg accounting and provide alternative versions for different scenarios, including one that caches each backing page's node in the chunk.

review_comments.author: Alexandre Ghiti
review_comments.summary: The author posted an initial patch series with performance data showing significant improvements over previous versions. They also mentioned an alternative version that caches each backing page's node in the chunk, but decided not to include it in this series.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sorry for the noise, too many emails at once, I'll add some delay.

Sorry again,

Alex

On 6/26/26 12:09, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
> This is version 2 of per-memcg-per-node kmem accounting.
>
> As asked by Joshua, I ran some microbenchmarks to check the impact of
> this fine grain accounting.
>
> TL;DR: There is a substantial impact (up to +337% on small percpu allocations)
> on a benchmark that loops over small percpu allocations. On the other hand,
> on a userspace program that creates a bpf percpu map, this cost is not visible.
>
> I followed Joshua's advice and now this version batches the memcg accounting:
> it improves the performance +337% vs +417% (v1) on 176 cores single node
> machine and +153% vs 206% (v1) on 80 cores 2 nodes machine.
>
> We can see that the overhead of this version scales linearly with the number of
> cpus (the number of nodes being small). This overhead comes mainly from
> vmalloc_to_page() so I have another variant (b) that decreases the impact even
> more (+131% vs +337% on 176 cores and +86% vs +153% on 80 cores) but I'm not
> sure the added complexity is needed so I did not send this version, let me know
> what you think.
>
> Performance
> ===========
>
> All benchmarks run in a memcg with __GFP_ACCOUNT.
>
> 1) BPF percpu map create/destroy, full series vs baseline kernel (two
>     boots, 176-CPU AMD EPYC, 1 NUMA node): the per-node accounting is lost
>     in the BPF syscall overhead, the delta is within noise (us/op):
>
>       size (B):    64     256    1024   4096   8192
>       delta:     -5.5%  -5.1%  -1.8%  -5.1%  -4.1%
>
> 2) In-kernel microbench that isolates the accounting cost: a tight
>     __alloc_percpu_gfp()/free_percpu() loop, __GFP_ACCOUNT on vs off on the
>     same boot (ACCT COST = on - off). The dominant cost on a many-CPU box
>     is discovering each backing page's real node (vmalloc_to_page() per
>     possible CPU). ACCT COST by value size:
>
>     176-CPU EPYC, 1 node
>       size (B):              64       256      1024     4096     8192
>        baseline (upstream)  +5.3%    +5.4%    +0.1%    -1.8%    -0.5%
>        v1 credit (per-page) +417.3%  +182.5%  +68.5%   +21.4%   +16.1%
>     a) per-node accounting  +337.8%  +141.8%  +36.1%   +11.9%   +6.8%
>     b) per-page nid cache   +131.3%  +53.7%   +10.5%   +0.9%    +2.0%
>     c) single-node fast     +12.6%   +12.1%   +3.5%    +6.6%    +0.7%
>
>     80-CPU Xeon Gold 6138, 2 nodes (fast path inactive)
>       size (B):              64       256      1024     4096     8192
>        baseline (upstream)  +1.2%    -3.8%    +12.4%   +1.2%    +0.5%   (noise)
>        v1 credit (per-page) +206.1%  +134.0%  +44.5%   +11.6%   +11.5%
>     a) per-node accounting  +153.2%  +64.7%   +19.4%   +4.2%    +5.9%
>     b) per-page nid cache   +86.5%   +45.5%   +14.7%   +1.8%    +1.6%
>
>     (a) this patchset without fast path for single node
>     (b) is an alternative version, not in this series, that caches each backing
>         page's node in the chunk so the walk is paid once per page instead of
>         once per allocation
>     (c) this patchset with fast path for single node
>
> Changes in v2
> =============
>
> - objcg lifetime: Shakeel's patch 1 now guarantees the lifetime of every
>    per-node objcg
> - dropped patch 5 and 6 since Shakeel's patch 2 replaces them
> - fixed the number of precharged pages (the v1 formula under-precharged)
> - per-node batching (Joshua's suggestion): accumulate the per-node bytes
>    first, then issue one account_kmem()/uncharge() per touched node =>
>    O(nodes) memcg ops instead of O(num_possible_cpus)
> - single-node fast path: skip the per-cpu node walk on single node machines
> - obj_exts metadata is now accounted per-node (walk its vmalloc pages)
>    rather than charged whole to one memcg (Shakeel's main v1 objection).
> - renamed obj_cgroup_get_nid() -> obj_cgroup_nid() (returns a borrowed RCU
>    pointer, no ref taken).
> - zswap: fixed the missing locking around the per-node objcg lookup (now
>    done under RCU + obj_cgroup_tryget()).
>
> This series pursues the work initiated by Joshua [1]. We need kernel
> memory to be accounted on a per-node basis in order to be able to know
> the memcg <-> physical memory association.
>
> This series takes advantage of the recently introduced per-node
> obj_cgroup and makes those obj_cgroup tied to their NUMA node.
>
> The bulk of the series is percpu per-node accounting: percpu
> "precharges" the memcg before we know the actual location of the pages
> it uses, so charging and accounting had to be split. All other kmem
> users (slab, __memcg_kmem_charge_page) are now handled directly by
> Shakeel's per-node obj_cgroup infrastructure this series sits on, so
> only percpu and zswap need explicit per-node work here (zswap support
> is limited because Joshua is working on it in parallel [3]).
>
> Thanks Joshua and Shakeel for the early feedback!
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260404033844.1892595-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
> [2] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/56c04b1c5d54f75ccdc12896df6c1ca35403ecc3.1772711148.git.zhengqi.arch@bytedance.com/
> [3] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260311195153.4013476-1-joshua.hahnjy@gmail.com/
>
> Functional Testing
> ==================
>
> - Tested with a percpu kmem self-test in an 8-node VM (2 nodes with CPUs,
>    6 memory-only). For each allocation it checks that every node is charged
>    and later uncharged the same number of bytes -- including a CPU-less node
>    that ends up holding the obj_exts metadata -- and that nothing is left
>    charged after teardown. All checks pass. (The self-test module is not
>    part of this series.)
>
> Alexandre Ghiti (7):
>    mm: percpu: fix obj_exts metadata charge size
>    mm: percpu: Split memcg charging and kmem accounting
>    mm: memcontrol: track MEMCG_KMEM per NUMA node
>    mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting
>    mm: percpu: per-node kmem accounting for obj_exts metadata
>    mm: percpu: skip the per-cpu node walk on single-node systems
>    mm: zswap: per-node kmem accounting for zswap/zsmalloc
>
> Shakeel Butt (2):
>    memcg: convert task->objcg to a per-node objcgs array
>    memcg: charge kmem pages and slab objects against per-node objcg
>
>   include/linux/memcontrol.h |  23 ++-
>   include/linux/mmzone.h     |   1 +
>   include/linux/sched.h      |   7 +-
>   include/linux/zsmalloc.h   |   2 +
>   mm/memcontrol.c            | 286 ++++++++++++++++++++++++++-----------
>   mm/percpu-internal.h       |   2 +-
>   mm/percpu.c                | 108 +++++++++++++-
>   mm/vmstat.c                |   1 +
>   mm/zsmalloc.c              |  11 ++
>   mm/zswap.c                 |  19 ++-
>   10 files changed, 361 insertions(+), 99 deletions(-)
>

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v2] smp: Use release stores for csd_lock_record() state
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: The patch aims to improve code readability by explicitly documenting synchronization pairs using release stores and load acquires.

review_comments.author: Dmitry Ilvokhin
review_comments.summary: Identified a potential issue with implicit synchronization pair inference and suggested adding explicit comments to clarify the pairing.

review_comments.author: Thomas Gleixner
review_comments.summary: Emphasized the importance of explicit documentation for synchronization pairs, citing kernel documentation guidelines.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jun 26 2026 at 16:45, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:
> On Fri, Jun 26, 2026 at 05:30:21PM +0100, Usama Arif wrote:
>> > Since v2 is specifically about documenting the pairing, it would be good
>> > to make it symmetric and add the comment on the acquire side in
>> > csd_lock_wait_toolong().
>> > 
>> 
>> Its already documented [1]
>> 
>> [1] https://elixir.bootlin.com/linux/v7.1.1/source/kernel/smp.c#L275

Can you please avoid these silly links to a random source tree and just
tell people kernel/smp.c line 275?

> It is documented to some extent, but it doesn't explicitly state which
> smp_store_release() the smp_load_acquire() pairs with. I think that's
> the main benefit of these comments: making the synchronization pair
> explicit so readers don't have to infer it.

It's mandatory according to Documentation:

    "Certain things should always be commented.  Uses of memory barriers should
     be accompanied by a line explaining why the barrier is necessary"

which means it needs to be at the place where the barrier is used and
that implies an explanation for the pairing. It's a pain having to do
detective work to figure it out.

Thanks,

        tglx

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add EM_WARN_ON_ONCE() to dump the extent map on a warning

version_history.subject: [PATCH] btrfs: add EM_WARN_ON_ONCE() to dump the extent map on a warning

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new macro EM_WARN_ON_ONCE() to the Btrfs file system, which dumps the contents of an extent map to the kernel log before triggering a warning and stack trace when certain consistency checks fail. The patch addresses a problem where WARN_ON() warnings were not providing enough information about the offending extent maps, leading to crashes in production kernels. The EM_WARN_ON_ONCE() macro is always compiled in, unlike other similar macros that are gated behind configuration options, ensuring that it can provide actionable context even on production systems.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2] sunrpc: hardcode pool_mode to pernode, remove other modes

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch removes the SVC_POOL_* enum and related code, hardcoding the pool mode to pernode (one pool per NUMA node) as it is considered the best choice today. The percpu mode, which created excessive pools, is removed along with its auto-selection logic. The module parameter and netlink interfaces are preserved for backward compatibility. If pool map allocation fails, service creation now fails instead of silently falling back to a single global pool.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that a server could end up with pools having no threads due to the way svc_set_num_threads() distributes threads across pools, and asked if there's a way for an admin to avoid empty pools on a server running fewer threads than NUMA nodes. He also pointed out that the documentation still presents the four pool modes as distinct, including global mode which is now removed.
review_comments.raw_body:
With one pool per node now mandatory, can a server end up with pools
that have no threads?  svc_set_num_threads() spreads nrservs evenly
across the pools:

net/sunrpc/svc.c:svc_set_num_threads() {
	unsigned int base = nrservs / serv->sv_nrpools;
	unsigned int remain = nrservs % serv->sv_nrpools;
	...
}

so when nrservs is smaller than the number of NUMA nodes with CPUs,
base is 0 and the trailing pools get zero threads.

Work is still routed to the per-node pool selected from the running
CPU:

net/sunrpc/svc_xprt.c:svc_xprt_enqueue() {
	pool = svc_pool_for_cpu(xprt->xpt_server);
	...
	lwq_enqueue(&xprt->xpt_ready, &pool->sp_xprts);
	svc_pool_wake_idle_thread(pool);
}

and svc_pool_wake_idle_thread() only consults that one pool's idle
list, with no fallback to a sibling pool.  If a connection lands on a
node whose pool has no threads, does its transport sit on sp_xprts and
never get serviced?

The old global default kept a single pool, so this configuration could
not arise without an explicit pernode/percpu selection.  With global
mode removed, is there a way left for an admin to avoid empty pools on
a server that runs fewer threads than it has NUMA nodes, or should the
thread distribution or the enqueue path guarantee work only reaches a
populated pool?

Separately, the pool_mode knob is preserved but every accepted value
now behaves as pernode.  Documentation/admin-guide/kernel-parameters.txt
still presents the four modes as distinct:

	auto	    the server chooses an appropriate mode
		    automatically using heuristics
	global	    a single global pool contains all CPUs
	percpu	    one pool for each CPU
	pernode	    one pool for each NUMA node (equivalent
		    to global on non-NUMA machines)

and states the option "will affect which CPUs will do NFS serving."
The patch should update this documentation.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: NeilBrown
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer NeilBrown suggested replacing the '!strncmp' construct in the patch with either 'match_string()' or 'sysfs_match_string()', citing a dislike for the former

The reviewer suggested initializing the count field of the svc_pool_map structure to 0 explicitly to make it clear there are no references, and also recommended renaming the count field to refcount for clarity.

Reviewer NeilBrown agreed that hardcoding pool_mode to pernode and removing other modes is a worthwhile change, but suggested that the minimum number of threads should be one per node instead of one total.
review_comments.raw_body:
"!strncmp" is one most disliked constructs....
What would you think of using match_string() or even
sysfs_match_string() ??

---

Could we make this
   m->count = 0

to make it crystal clear that there are now no references?
It would be nice to rename ->count to ->refcount or similar
so it is obvious what is being counted, but that doesn't need to be part
of this change.

---

I think this is a good a worthwhile change.
Regarding Chucks comments, I think a minimum of one thread per node,
rather than a minimum of one thread total, is the sensible approach.
Of course if there exist memory-only nodes, they wouldn't need a thread.

Thanks,
NeilBrown

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Chuck Lever noted that the patch does not handle the case where transport work is scheduled on a memory-only pool, which would have no execution engine even if threads are provided.
sentiment_signals: lack of consideration for edge cases
review_comments.raw_body:
What happens when transport work is scheduled on a memory-only
pool? Obviously there's no execution engine there, even if we
do provide one or more threads.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about preserving userland's ability to dictate thread counts in each pool, and proposed a belt-and-braces approach to handle this: assigning at least one thread to each node when auto-distributing threads, and adding a fallback in the queueing path to warn about sub-optimal configurations.
review_comments.raw_body:
The hitch here is that we have traditionally allowed userland to
dictate how many threads each pool gets. You're allowed to send down an
array like this with (4 pools):

     0 128 0 0

That would give you exactly that layout (128 threads in pool 2 and none
elsewhere). I think we can continue to honor that by saying that if you
set a thread count of 0 in a pool then we should not spawn any threads
there.

Because of this, I favor a belt and bracers approach:

1/ when min-threads is set, assign at least one thread to each node
that has a cpu as a minimum, but only when we are auto-distributing
threads between nodes (the typical case). This allows us to preserve
the wishes of userland when it sends down a more complex pool array. We
should not override the user's wishes in that case.

2/ add in a fallback in the queueing path so that when there are no
threads in a node, we queue it to a populated one. We could even add a
pr_notice_once() that fires in that case to warn that the configuration
is sub-optimal. The cost is an extra check for sp_nrthreads == 0 when
enqueueing, but it should almost always come back false when things are
properly configured.

-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledges that the removal of pool mode knobs may be premature and expresses uncertainty about the guidelines for such removals.

The author acknowledged a concern about renaming counters in the patch and agreed to leave it as future cleanup.

The author addressed a concern about the pool map initialization, explaining that it is unconditionally called from svc_new_thread() for non-pooled services created via svc_create(), and thus m->count == 0 and m->pool_to == NULL.

The author is addressing a concern about queueing to pools (nodes) that have no threads in them, which could help catch misconfigurations. They agree this is part of the solution and suggest another step to improve it.
review_comments.raw_body:
I'll make this more vague. I'm not sure what the guidance is on removal
of knobs like this. A few years, I guess?

---

Yep, fixed. I didn't rename the counter though. That can be future
cleanup.

---

Yes, it can. It's called unconditionally from svc_new_thread(), which
runs for non-pooled services created via svc_create() (e.g. lockd) that
never call svc_pool_map_get(). In that case m->count == 0 and
m->pool_to == NULL.

---

I responded to Chuck's reply on this point. I think that's part of a
solution, but we probably also need to avoid queueing to pools (nodes)
that have no threads in them, to help catch misconfigurations.

Thanks for the review!
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/3] nfsd: inter-server copy fixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/3] nfsd: fix cpntf publish race in nfs4_init_cp_state
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a race condition in the NFSv4 server (nfsd) code, specifically in the nfs4_init_cp_state function. The issue arises when two concurrent requests are made to publish a copy notify state ID, causing a NULL dereference and resulting in a server crash. To fix this, the patch folds the cs_type assignment and list_add operations into a single critical section, ensuring that the cp_list is properly initialized before it's accessed by other threads. Additionally, the patch updates the _free_cpntf_state_locked function to use list_del_init instead of list_del, preventing any stale unlinks from causing issues.

review_comments.author: NeilBrown

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/3] nfsd: fix UAF in cpntf statelist drain
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS server's cpntf statelist drain function. The bug occurs when a concurrent holder increments the reference count of a state, causing the drain to re-decrement the same entry on subsequent iterations and burn the original reference held by the concurrent holder. To fix this, the patch rewrites the drain function to unconditionally revoke each entry before dropping the parent's reference, ensuring that the drain terminates in one pass per entry regardless of the reference count.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/3] nfsd: fix UAF in async copy cancel and shutdown
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's async copy cancel and shutdown functionality. The issue arises when an async copy is canceled or shut down, causing the reaper to access a freed filecache object. To fix this, the patch modifies the find_async_copy function to clear the copy's reference count under the async lock and remove it from the async copies list before calling nfsd4_stop_copy. Additionally, the patch updates the cancel and shutdown functions to call nfs4_put_copy after nfsd4_stop_copy to release the list-membership reference held by the reaper.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 1/3] iomap: add ->iomap_next() and iomap_process() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new callback function called ->iomap_next() to the iomap iteration mechanism, which replaces the existing ->iomap_begin() and ->iomap_end() callbacks. The new callback allows performance-critical callers to inline the iteration with a fixed callback, improving efficiency. A helper function called iomap_process() is also added to simplify the logic needed in an ->iomap_next() implementation. This change enables filesystems to be converted one at a time from using the legacy ->iomap_begin()/->iomap_end() path to the new ->iomap_next() callback.

The change does not introduce any functional changes but rather refactors the existing implementation. This is a step towards migrating all XFS iomap operations to use the ->iomap_next() callback.

series_items.subject: [RFC PATCH v1 3/3] xfs: example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks
series_items.patch_summary: This patch is an example of devirtualizing buffered write iomap callbacks in the XFS file system. It removes indirect calls by inlining callback functions and allows the compiler to make direct calls, reducing overhead. The patch modifies several files, including iomap/buffered-io.c, iomap/iter.c, xfs/xfs_file.c, and others, adding new functions and modifying existing ones to use the devirtualized callbacks. The goal is to improve performance by eliminating indirect function calls.

reviewer noted that the patch does not fully align with their original in-iter direction and provided a specific example of the design model difference they are concerned about

Reviewer Gao Xiang noted that the recent iomap implementation is still callback-based and requires arranging mapping logic into unique callbacks, which can be inflexible for filesystems needing additional information. He suggested a high-level fs helper library to wrap up page cache management, IO management, and other operations, allowing for more flexibility in handling different types of I/O.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Joanne,

On 2026/6/26 05:33, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Thanks for the reply.

I partially agree since it's much closer, but what I meant is
the design model difference as below.

---

If you take a look at the initial commit of iomap ae259a9c8593
("fs: introduce iomap infrastructure"), the starting point was
to provide `struct iomap_ops` (two callbacks) to get the filesystem
extent mapping and unique entrypoints: iomap_file_buffered_write(),
iomap_page_mkwrite() for buffer writes (write and mmap write).

And then iomap applies to DAX, direct I/O, writeback, buffer read,
etc.  basically for block-based filesystems, and that is why
`struct iomap_ops` is always _one important part of iomap core_
until FUSE adapted.


But if we take a look at the overall requirement, I think what we
need is to provide an easy-use high-level fs helper library to
wrap up the following stuffs:

  1. page cache management (mainly sub-page tracking) and DAX pages;

  2. IO management (buffer I/O, direct I/O, DAX access);

  3. others (like fiemap, bmap, lseek, etc.)


For example, most block filesystems could use them of all, and for
FUSE and networking filesystem, basically they only need 1 and
maybe partial 3.

The recent implementation (including ->iomap_next()), is still
a callback-based model, the main loop is inside `fs/iomap` itself,
and you have to:

  - still arrange the mapping logic into an unique callback, which
is .iomap_next() now, if the mapping logic needs more information
rather than `struct inode *`, they have to add private pointer
somewhere.

  - Take a recent example (buffered read refactoring), need
    `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` for FUSE and maybe other future
    networking filesystems to callback into their filesystem to
    provide new I/O contexts and submit I/Os.

But actually take a simple example of iomap_read_folio, you:
could change into (just a very rough sketch):

  xxxfs_read_folio {
    struct xxxxfs_private priv;

    iomap_iter(&iter) {   /// it could be a macro
        if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);

        // to maintain the page cache <-> struct iomap_folio_state
        // and handle some type of mapping
        if (iomap_read_folio_iter(&iter) == NEED_NEW_IO_CONTEXT) {
            xxxfs_new_io (&iter, &priv);
        }
    }
    if (xxxfs_if_any_available_io(&iter, &priv))
	xxxfs_submit_io(&iter, &priv);
}

In this model, I really think `struct iomap_read_folio_ctx` and
similar doesn't need any more (another set of callback avoidness),
and both xxxfs_map_extent() and xxxfs_new_io()/ xxxfs_submit_io()
can pass in their per-fs private without need to bother
the iomap-core to add various `private` pointers.

and in the iomap_iter() loop you can omit/change/add any part if
it doesn't fulfill to the filesystem timing.

As for "a lot of duplicate code", I think with proper new designs,
the common part could be wrapped up into macros (for example,
for bdev filesystems, xxxfs_new_io() and xxxfs_submit_io() calls
can be wrapped up into macros, and only leave:

	if (iomap_iter_outofrange(&iter))
		xxxfs_map_extent(&iter, &priv);"

can be customized for each fs as the common macro helpers.

It's just my rough thought, as I said in the previous year, I
still don't have a strong tendency but it's my suggestion over
these years.

Anyway since you're actively working/changing on iomap, so
it's fine to just respect your ideas/efforts if you don't
think it's worth, but as a record of my thoughts here again.

Thanks,
Gao Xiang

He requested a proof-of-concept from Joanne Koong to gauge interest.
review_comments.raw_body:
This does sound like a lot of work, and I'm not a 100% sold on
the benefits, although getting rid of callbacks would be nice.

I'd be tempted to say that as a first step we should move the the
single callback, as a second stop (after converting everyone to
the single callback) to inline for performance critical path, and
then see if we can massage it into a pure iterator without much
downside instead of trying to do everything at once.

As you seem to have the strongest opinion here, I'd love to see
a proof of concept from you to get everyone excited.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: page_alloc: __GFP_FS lockdep annotation for direct compaction
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds lockdep annotations to the direct compaction function in the Linux kernel's page allocation code, specifically for __GFP_FS passing errors. This is done to future-proof against potential issues and provide peace of mind by catching any errors that may occur when compaction enters filesystem paths. The annotation is similar to what has been present in direct reclaim code for a while.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: compaction: support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch modifies the Linux kernel's compaction code to support non-movable compaction for pageblock requests. It relaxes the restrictions on type mixing and pollution, allowing whole-block requests to scan blocks of other types. The exception is CMA (Contiguous Memory Allocation) blocks, which remain strictly typed due to their sticky nature. The patch aims to improve the efficiency of defrag_mode by enabling non-movable direct compaction, making it more useful for producing whole blocks that can be converted wholesale on allocation.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] mm: page_alloc: move capture_control to the page allocator
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the per-task capture_control to the page allocator, allowing it to carry allocation-side information that compaction's compact_control does not. The capture_control is passed through try_to_compact_pages() and compact_zone_order() instead of a bare struct page **. This change is necessary because defrag_mode promotes sub-block allocations to pageblock-order compaction, which targets the larger order while capture should remain at the original allocation order. The patch makes no functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] mm: page_alloc: fix non-movable reclaim storm in defrag_mode
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a problem where non-movable page allocations cause excessive reclaim storms in defrag_mode, leading to performance issues and OOM triggers. The issue arises when non-movable requests retry on reclaim, causing them to spin and over-reclaim excessively. To fix this, the patch sends non-movable requests directly into pageblock reclaim/compaction, allowing them to help move pages along and meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. This is a temporary solution until a longer-term plan can be implemented to have all requests, including movable ones, contribute to block creation and reduce allocation latencies.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes reclaim storms in defrag_mode by making non-movable requests participate in pageblock production in the allocator slowpath. The changes involve small adjustments to the allocator and compaction code, including three prep patches and a fix. The series has been tested against an affected workload for two weeks and restored the OOM kill rate to baseline.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Corrected the commit hash for the fixup patch. The reviewer pointed out an error in the commit message, specifically that the referenced commit was not found in the tree.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri Jun 26, 2026 at 2:21 PM EDT, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> As we deployed defrag_mode into Meta production, pressure spikes and
> excessive swapping were observed on some workloads. Tracing confirmed
> that this is unmovable/reclaimable requests spinning in the allocator
> and direct reclaim, causing excessive amounts of swap.
>
> The initial plan for defrag_mode was to rely on kswapd/kcompactd to
> produce blocks, and if those are overwhelmed under high pressure, let
> the allocator fall back (__rmqueue_steal()) after its retry loops.
> However, that retrying results in more reclaim on some of these
> workloads than we'd hoped, sometimes excessively so, spurred on by the
> !costly order conditions in should_reclaim_retry().
>
> The storms are dependent on the request type. Reclaim will inevitably
> make room in existing movable blocks, since that's where the LRU pages
> live. So if movable requests retry on reclaim, they make progress.
>
> When non-movable requests spin in reclaim that isn't productive. They
> cannot use the individually freed pages, and the process is unlikely
> to accidentally free whole blocks to meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar.
> They spin and overreclaim excessively, which tanks performance and
> triggers userspace guards like swap exhaustion or pressure based OOM.
>
> To fix this, send non-movable requests, regardless of order, into
> pageblock reclaim/compaction. This way, they help move things along to
> meet the ALLOC_NOFRAGMENT bar. After this patch, the reclaim storms
> and excess OOM rates are no longer observed in production.
>
> The longer-term plan is still to have all requests, including the
> movable ones, help make blocks to spread the cost of defragmenting
> more evenly and fairly; combined with proper watermarking to reduce
> allocation latencies in the common case. However, doing this naively
> unearths scaling and concurrency limitations in compaction that need
> to be addressed first. Promoting just non-movables for now is the
> minimally viable bug fix for the above issue.
>
> Fixes: f38356df6474 ("mm: page_alloc: introduce defrag_mode")

Should be
Fixes: e3aa7df331bc ("mm: page_alloc: defrag_mode").
Since I cannot find f38356df6474 in the tree.

> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

-- 
Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

version_history.subject: [PATCH v4 0/5] mm/memcontrol, page_counter: move stock from mem_cgroup to page_counter

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] mm/page_counter: introduce per-page_counter stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a per-page_counter stock, which allows each page counter to have its own cache of pre-charged pages. This addresses issues with the current implementation, including random eviction of memcgs when there are more than 7 competing for CPU resources, and tight coupling between stock and memcg. The new design enables separate stock caches for other page counters and simplifies charging logic. Stock usage and refills now happen via lockless atomic operations, eliminating the need for asynchronous workqueues.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] mm/memcontrol: flatten try_charge_memcg control flow
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the try_charge_memcg function in mm/memcontrol.c to simplify its control flow by separating the logic for memsw and memory page counters into distinct paths. The change is made to improve readability, but does not introduce any functional changes. With this flattening, it will be easier to follow the control flow when page_counter_try_charge becomes stock-aware.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] mm/page_counter: introduce page_counter_try_charge_stock()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, page_counter_try_charge_stock(), which is a variant of the existing page_counter_try_charge() function. It first attempts to charge from per-cpu stock, then tries to refill the stock by overcharging up to the batch size if necessary, and finally charges exactly the requested number of pages if all else fails. The function reports back the number of pages charged to the caller, allowing for more efficient handling of memory limits.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock
series_items.patch_summary: This patch converts the memory control (memcg) system to use page_counter_stock instead of its own stock handling logic. This change removes special casing for spinning and refilling, allows for failure during allocation if there's not enough memory, and updates drain_all_stock and memcg_hotplug_cpu_dead to use the new stock drain variant. As a result, memsw and memory page counters now have independent stocks, which can lead to transient discrepancies in reported usage.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Usama Arif noted that page_counter_try_charge_stock() can succeed by consuming memsw stock and charging 0 new pages, but the memory-failure path unconditionally uncharges nr_pages from memsw, leading to a real memsw usage decrement even in case of failed allocation. He requested a transactional rollback.
review_comments.raw_body:
This needs a transactional rollback. page_counter_try_charge_stock() can
succeed by consuming memsw stock and charging 0 new pages, but the
memory-failure path unconditionally uncharges nr_pages from memsw.
That turns a failed allocation into a real memsw usage decrement.

review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that the swapoff path needs to drop the per-vswap spinlock before calling try_to_unmap(), agreed to restructure in v2, but did not provide a specific plan for addressing this issue.

Author is unsure about whether the check for cgroup v1 with memsw is unconditional and asks for clarification on reviewer's concern that taking a charge via stock but uncharging with a hierarchical page_counter walk could cause the stock to grow without bound.

Thank you for reviewing the patch : -)

[...snip...]

Hmmmmmmmmmm....... I'm not sure.

At this point in the code, we are either (1) using cgroup v1 with memsw
and charged successfully, or (2) not using cgroup v1 with memsw. So I'm
not sure if this really is unconditional, we're just distinguishing
between cases (1) and (2) by checking if we're using cgroupv1.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] mm/memcontrol: remove unused memcg_stock code
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unused code related to handling 'memcg_stock' in the Linux kernel's memory control (mm/memcontrol) module. The removal is possible because all relevant logic has been moved to the 'page_counter_stock' module. The patch preserves the 'FLUSHING_CACHED_CHARGE' flag, which is still needed by another part of the code. The changes involve deleting 186 lines of code from a single file (mm/memcontrol.c).

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series moves the per-cpu stock from struct mem_cgroup to page_counter, reducing memory footprint and eliminating locking and synchronous work_on_cpu. The new implementation uses atomic operations for stock management and allows for independent stock management for additional page_counters in each memcg. This change simplifies memcontrol code and enables the addition of a new page_counter to track toptier usage. However, it introduces a potential increase in memory overcharging on large machines with many cgroups.

review_comments.author: Joshua Hahn (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing concerns raised by Sashiko about potential issues in commits 3/5 and 4/5 of the patch series, specifically regarding unbounded growth of per-CPU stock, memory stranding bounds, cacheline bouncing, and premature OOM kills. The author agrees to add checks to prevent unbounded growth, restricts batch size, and addresses cacheline bouncing by adding a volatile read before atomic_xchg(). A new v5 will be spun up with these changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello,

I just want to address a few things that Sashiko raised. I think there
are definitely some improvements that I can make as Sashiko suggested.

In commit 3/5 mm/page_counter: introduce page_counter_try_charge_stock()
Sashiko raises two concerns.

"Can the per-CPU stock grow unboundedly beyond counter->batch pages here?"

I think this is true. I went back to the original stock design and saw
that when the stock is greater than the batch size, it just drains all
of it (since this means we raced). I can add the same check so that we
never grow beyond the batch size. This should also help with the point
below.

"Does moving the per-CPU cache from a single shared stock to a per-page_counter
stock fundamentally change the memory stranding bounds?"

This is true, and I addressed this in the cover letter. Yes, the worst-case
upper bound grows by quite a bit, but it is difficult to hit that limit
since it would require a memcg process to be scheduled on all the CPUs,
and strand memory there via the stock. Nonetheless, restricting the
batch size should make this worst-case a bit better.

In commit 4/5 mm/memcontrol: convert memcg to use page_counter_stock()
Sashiko also raises two concerns.

"Could this synchronous loop cause cacheline bouncing and premature OOM kills?"

Sashiko is referring to the memcg-cpu iteration we do where we drain the
stock of an entire descendant completely. I also addressed this in the
cover letter and that I couldn't really reproduce the issue in my
testing. I addressed this every version but it seems like Sashiko does
not read the cover letter :' (

"Would doing a volatile read to check if
the stock has pages before calling atomic_xchg() help mitigate this?"

This one I agree with, I'll add:

if (!atomic_read(&stock->nr_pages))
	return;
nr_pages = atomic_xchg(&stock->nr_pages, 0);

And hopefully we can avoid most of the unnecsesary races (of course the
value can still change in between the read and the atomic_xchg but it's
just a best-effort optimization)

So I'll spin up a v5. One thing I'm going back and forth in my mind
is whether we want separate stocked and non-stocked variants, or if
that should just happen transparently within the calls.

Thanks again Sashiko!
Joshua

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort
[PATCH v4 4/4] arm64: escalate smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI as a last resort

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new mechanism to the Linux kernel on arm64 architecture to handle CPU wedging by escalating smp_send_stop() to an SDEI NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) as a last resort. When a CPU is wedged with interrupts masked, it ignores stop IPIs and without pseudo-NMI there's no NMI IPI to escalate to. The patch introduces a third rung in smp_send_stop() that signals an SDEI event 0 at the remaining online CPUs, which firmware delivers regardless of the target's DAIF (Debug Access Interface Flag). This allows the CPU to reach a wedged CPU and acknowledge by going offline. The stop bookkeeping is also folded into a shared function arm64_nmi_cpu_stop() for consistency.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function called sdei_event_signal() to the arm_sdei firmware driver, which signals a software-signalled event (event 0) to another processor element (PE). The function is designed to be safe to call from non-maskable interrupt (NMI) or crash context and takes no locks. It uses the SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL call to deliver the event NMI-like even when interrupts are masked on the target PE. The patch also updates the include files to reflect the new function.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new mechanism to deliver NMI (Non-Maskable Interrupt) across CPUs on arm64 architecture using SDEI (System Description Event Infrastructure). review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author is addressing concerns about the performance cost of pseudo-NMI on arm64 systems. They acknowledge that previous numbers were unreliable and provide fresh measurements from NVIDIA Grace (Neoverse V2) showing a measurable cost, but claim it's a constant per-syscall tax rather than a contention effect.

Author acknowledges that FEAT_NMI is the long-term solution, but expresses concern about a decade-long gap where existing silicon will not have either FEAT_NMI or pseudo-NMI, and argues that their SDEI-based patch is a minimal use of a deprecated interface to cover this gap. Author asks for an alternative solution if the current approach is unacceptable.
review_comments.raw_body:
Full disclosure: the ~66% figures come from internal testing about a year ago.
I no longer have the details of the machine it ran on and can't confirm whether
ICC_CTLR_EL1.PMHE was set there -- it may well have been. I shouldn't have
carried those numbers forward without being able to stand behind them, so
please disregard them.

Here are fresh numbers from NVIDIA Grace (Neoverse V2). Importantly, this
box reports:

  GICv3: Pseudo-NMIs enabled using relaxed ICC_PMR_EL1 synchronisation

i.e. PMHE == 0, so the synchronising DSB on the unmask path is already
patched to a NOP (ARM64_HAS_GIC_PRIO_RELAXED_SYNC). What's left is the
floor cost of PMR-based masking itself plus the PMR save/restore on
exception entry/exit -- not the DSB. So this is the case Catalin asked
about (DSB omitted), and there is still a measurable cost.

A trivial single-threaded gettid() loop (1e6 calls, median of 5,
performance governor, ASLR off):

  pseudo_nmi=0 (DAIF):       178.4 ns/call
  pseudo_nmi=1 (PMR):        252.5 ns/call
  delta:                     +74.1 ns/call  (~230-250 cycles)
                             +41.5% wall time / 0.706 throughput

  --- u-bench.c ---
  #include <unistd.h>
  #include <sys/syscall.h>
  #include <time.h>
  #include <stdio.h>
  int main(void) {
          struct timespec a, b;
          clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &a);
          for (long i = 0; i < 1000000; i++)
                  syscall(SYS_gettid);
          clock_gettime(CLOCK_MONOTONIC, &b);
          printf("%f ns\n", (b.tv_sec-a.tv_sec)*1e9 + (b.tv_nsec-a.tv_nsec));
          return 0;
  }

will-it-scale agrees independently. sched_yield (ops/s, median of 5):

                      1 task       72 tasks
  pseudo_nmi=0      3,195,656    230,824,534
  pseudo_nmi=1      2,253,753    163,914,837
  ratio                0.705          0.710

The ratio is flat across the whole 1-to-72 sweep, so -- relevant to the
scalability question -- it's a constant per-syscall tax, not a contention
effect. The impact tracks syscall/exception density: page_fault1, a more
realistic workload, stays within ~5%.

---

I understand FEAT_NMI is the long-term answer, and I'm not arguing against
deprecating SDEI. My concern is the gap in between. By our estimate it's
10+ years before the last non-FEAT_NMI machine retires from the fleet --
for scale, we're still running Skylake today. So there's roughly a
decade where a large installed base has neither FEAT_NMI nor affordable
pseudo-NMI, and no way to reach a DAIF-masked CPU for an all-CPU
backtrace or to capture a wedged CPU in a crash dump. That's the
functional gap this series tries to cover.

Given the deprecation direction, I deliberately kept the SDEI footprint as
small as I could. The series adds no new firmware interface and no vendor
SMC -- it uses only the standard software-signalled event (event 0) via
SDEI_EVENT_SIGNAL, which is already present on these systems for
firmware-first RAS (APEI/GHES). And SDEI is only ever invoked in a "bad
state": to deliver a backtrace signal to a CPU that a normal IPI can't
reach, or to stop a CPU that ignored the stop IPIs. Nothing on any hot or
steady-state path touches it.

If even that minimal use is unacceptable on a deprecated interface, I'd
rather know now and redirect the effort -- but I'd appreciate a pointer to
what should cover this gap for existing silicon in the meantime.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Breno Leitao
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Breno Leitao agrees with the patch and suggests maintaining it downstream if it doesn't get upstreamed, citing the trade-offs of pseudo-NMI performance overhead, losing debuggability by disabling NMI entirely, or carrying downstream patches as the least disruptive option for their business needs.
review_comments.raw_body:
I completely agree. Should these patches unfortunately not make it upstream,
my plan is to maintain them downstream at Meta kernels until the Meta fleet
no longer contains any non-FEAT_NMI hosts.

The trade-offs under consideration are:

	1) Accept the performance overhead from pseudo-NMI
	2) Lose debuggability by disabling NMI entirely
	3) Maintain downstream patches

Of these three options, carrying downstream patches appears to be the least
disruptive path for our business needs.

review_comments.author: Catalin Marinas
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Catalin Marinas questioned whether firmware can handle a PSCI CPU_OFF call from the SDEI handler, which is one of the required calls for this mechanism to work.
review_comments.raw_body:
Just to understand, your firmware cannot cope with a PSCI CPU_OFF from
the SDEI handler? This is one of the required calls to be supported.

-- 
Catalin

review_comments.author: Catalin Marinas
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that a dsb(ishst) instruction should be used instead of smp_wmb() to ensure proper ordering, citing the GIC IPI code as an example and expressing uncertainty about whether this is mandated by the SDEI spec.
review_comments.raw_body:
The smp_wmb() is not sufficient here. In the GIC IPI code we use a
dsb(ishst). It should be similar here. I think TF-A does this already
but it's unclear from the SDEI spec that it is mandated.

-- 
Catalin

review_comments.author: Catalin Marinas
review_comments.summary: The reviewer, Catalin Marinas, questioned whether a barrier is needed in the patch, citing that backtrace_mask won't be visible without it, and referenced a similar concern raised in patch 4.
review_comments.raw_body:
Does this need a barrier as in patch 4? Sashiko spotted that
backtrace_mask won't be visible without a dsb(ishst). Again, as per my
comment in patch 4, we get away with this because of the TF-A
implementation but that's not guaranteed (we could ask for the SDEI spec
to be updated assuming all firmware implementations do this).

-- 
Catalin

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author investigated a hang issue on Grace that occurred when calling PSCI CPU_OFF after completing an SDEI event, and found that it persisted even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset first. They acknowledged this as a separate issue from the main patch series and stated that it's only necessary for crash kernel reclamation, not required for the series.
review_comments.raw_body:
I did chase it a fair bit. Bisecting on Grace: completing the event and
parking (no CPU_OFF) works, and so does the stack-switch + C-call setup
on its own. The hang only appears once I call PSCI CPU_OFF after the
event -- and it persists even with DAIF masked and the GIC PMR reset
first, so it isn't leftover interrupt/priority state from the dispatch.
It's a silent wedge: no TF-A exception report, nothing after the
last console line.

But I have not tried calling CPU_OFF directly, without completing the
event. I assumed it is required. Will give it a try when I have time.

Either way this is a side quest: it only lets a crash kernel reclaim the
stopped CPU. The dump itself is complete, so it's nice-to-have, not
required for the series.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a technical issue in patch 3/4, agreeing to add a barrier (dsb(ishst)) to the backtrace path to ensure correct ordering of operations.
review_comments.raw_body:
You're right, I'll switch it to dsb(ishst) to match gic_ipi_send_mask().

And as you pointed, the xame fix is needed in 3/4: the backtrace path
publishes backtrace_mask before signalling and currently has no barrier
at all (as the bot spotted), so it needs the dsb(ishst) there too.

I'll also drop the now-incorrect "cumulative wmb" comment.

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov
